hypothesis: Papulacandin B is the main and generally most potent component in the reported MIC panel
text_quote: B is most potent in Table 3 for most Candida and yeast rows.
curator_note: Use as early whole-cell activity ranking evidence only. No enzyme IC50 in this paper.

hypothesis: Papulacandin A is generally less active than Papulacandin B in the MIC panel
text_quote: A is about one half as active as B in text and Table 3.
curator_note: Do not infer structural cause yet because complete structures are not given here.

hypothesis: Papulacandin C is generally less active than Papulacandin B in the MIC panel
text_quote: C is about one half as active as B in text and Table 3.
curator_note: Do not infer structural cause yet because complete structures are not given here.

hypothesis: Papulacandin D has lower and more strain-dependent whole-cell activity than B
text_quote: D is described as lower activity and Table 3 reports weaker MIC values for many strains.
curator_note: D remains important later because simplified Papulacandin D derivatives are synthetically useful.

hypothesis: Papulacandin E is described as a minor lower-activity component but numeric MIC values are not tabulated here
text_quote: D and E are described as minor components with lower activity.
curator_note: Keep E out of numeric activity modeling until a paper with exact activity is curated.

hypothesis: Papulacandin B may act on yeast cell-wall structural glucan and shows partial cross-resistance with echinocandin B
text_quote: Text reports evidence for structural glucan site of action and partial cross resistance with echinocandin B.
curator_note: Mechanistic clue only. No direct enzyme assay in this paper.

hypothesis: Papulacandin B directly inhibits alkali-insoluble glucan synthesis in yeast spheroplasts
text_quote: Spheroplast ED50 values are 0.16 ug/ml in S. cerevisiae and 0.03 ug/ml in C. albicans.
curator_note: Mechanistic activity entry for target-linked modeling; not a structural analog comparison.

hypothesis: Papulacandin B does not behave like polyene membrane-disrupting antibiotics
text_quote: Papulacandin B caused negligible potassium release up to 50 ug/ml unlike amphotericin B at 2.5 ug/ml.
curator_note: Potassium release is a mechanism counter-screen rather than an antifungal potency endpoint.

hypothesis: A Papulacandin-B-resistant C. albicans CA2 strain shows much weaker glucan-synthesis inhibition
text_quote: CA2 spheroplast ED50 is 2.5 ug/ml versus 0.03 ug/ml for K1133.
curator_note: Useful for resistance phenotype annotation.

hypothesis: Human serum reduces apparent Papulacandin B activity in C. albicans systems
text_quote: K1133 MIC shifts from 0.1 to 2.6 ug/ml with 10% human serum; spheroplast ED50 shifts from 0.03 to 0.8 ug/ml.
curator_note: Keep serum and serum-free data separated during modeling.

hypothesis: Spheroplast assays suggest higher apparent sensitivity than intact-cell assays
text_quote: S. cerevisiae ED50 changes from 5 to 0.16 ug/ml and C. albicans from 0.5 to 0.03 ug/ml.
curator_note: Interpret as exposure or barrier context rather than a new compound property.

hypothesis: Echinocandin B appears to share a related glucan-synthesis inhibitory phenotype
text_quote: CA2 is cross-resistant to echinocandin B and echinocandin B selectively inhibits glucan synthesis in spheroplasts.
curator_note: Mechanism-family context for later echinocandin comparison.

hypothesis: The long fatty acid residue is required for target-site glucan-synthesis inhibition
text_quote: Degradation product I has ED50 0.3 ug/ml but product II is >5 ug/ml and MIC >128 ug/ml.
curator_note: Compare paper compounds 8 and 9; this is one of the strongest SAR anchors in NO.3.

hypothesis: The short fatty acid is not essential for glucan ED50 but is important for whole-cell MIC
text_quote: Degradation product I has ED50 0.3 ug/ml and MIC >128 ug/ml.
curator_note: Interpreted as target activity retained but target access or whole-cell conversion impaired.

modification: galactose and short fatty acid removal
hypothesis: Neither the short fatty acid nor galactose is essential for biological activity at the target site
text_quote: Papulacandin D lacks short fatty acid and galactose but has ED50 0.13 ug/ml and MIC 1-2 ug/ml.
curator_note: Papulacandin D remains active and is proposed as a simplified starting material.

hypothesis: Hydrogenation of fatty acid residues reduces activity and strongly weakens whole-cell MIC
text_quote: Tetradecahydropapulacandin B has ED50 0.6 ug/ml and MIC >128 ug/ml.
curator_note: Octahydropapulacandin D also shows weaker ED50 and MIC than Papulacandin D.

hypothesis: Nona-acetylation of Papulacandin B abolishes measurable activity
text_quote: Papulacandin B nona-acetate has ED50 >5 ug/ml and MIC >128 ug/ml.
curator_note: Likely combines target-binding and exposure effects; do not over-interpret mechanism from acetylation alone.

hypothesis: Glucose position 6 substitutions are generally tolerated
text_quote: Compounds 11-14 show ED50 0.2-0.5 ug/ml and MIC 0.2-2 ug/ml.
curator_note: Series-level rule based on compounds 11-14.

hypothesis: Several aromatic position 10 substitutions preserve activity
text_quote: Compounds 19 and 21-23 show ED50 0.1-0.5 ug/ml and MIC 0.2-0.5 ug/ml.
curator_note: Position 10 appears more permissive than bulky 12-O substitution in this set.

hypothesis: Larger substituents at aromatic position 12 reduce glucan-synthesis and whole-cell activity
text_quote: Compound 17 has ED50 2 ug/ml and MIC 12 ug/ml; compound 18 has ED50 >5 and MIC >128 ug/ml.
curator_note: The paper explicitly contrasts larger 12 substituents with smaller methyl substitution.

hypothesis: Substitutions at aromatic position 11 tend to reduce biological activity
text_quote: Compounds 24-29 show ED50 0.2-3 ug/ml and MIC 0.2-6 ug/ml.
curator_note: Effect is not uniform; preserve individual activity rows for modeling.

hypothesis: Papulacandin D is a useful simplified scaffold for further modification
text_quote: Papulacandin D retains ED50 0.13 ug/ml and MIC 1-2 ug/ml while lacking short fatty acid and galactose.
curator_note: This is a design implication stated by the paper rather than a direct synthetic route.

hypothesis: Papulacandins can show strong serum-free Candida activity but weak in vivo translation
text_quote: Papulacandin B MIC 0.05 ug/mL and ED50 80 mg/kg; paper states high in vitro activity but weaker in vivo
curator_note: Use this row to avoid the wrong shorthand that Papulacandins are cell-inactive.

hypothesis: Polar 10-O ether can improve in vivo activity without losing Candida MIC
text_quote: Compound 8f has MIC 0.1 ug/mL and ED50 30 mg/kg; discussion states it is about three times as active as Papulacandin B
curator_note: 8f is one of the clearest early leads for improving the in vivo gap.

hypothesis: Position 10 is more permissive than position 12 for O-alkylation
text_quote: 10-O derivative 8a has MIC 0.2 ug/mL while 12-O derivative 5a has MIC 0.4 ug/mL; discussion favors 10 over 12
curator_note: Keep as a site-level rule and preserve individual rows because in vivo values vary strongly by substituent.

hypothesis: Increasing 12-O alkyl size reduces Candida MIC and in vivo efficacy
text_quote: Compound 5c has MIC 12.5 ug/mL and ED50 >300 mg/kg
curator_note: This extends the NO.3 12-O rule into the NO.4 in vivo dataset; NO.4 compound 5c is merged with NO.3 PAPU-0018.

hypothesis: Large 10-12-diethers are often poor for Candida whole-cell activity
text_quote: Compound 6c has MIC >100 ug/mL and no useful ED50 reported
curator_note: The di-O series should not be treated as equivalent to tolerated single 10-O substitution; NO.4 compound 6c is merged with NO.3 PAPU-0019.

hypothesis: 11-aminomethylene style substitution does not solve in vivo efficacy and can reduce MIC
text_quote: Compound 9 has MIC >100 ug/mL and ED50 >300 mg/kg
curator_note: This rule is distinct from 11-aminoacyl derivatives which behave better.

hypothesis: Selected 11-aminoacyl derivatives can improve in vivo activity while keeping useful Candida MIC
text_quote: Compound 15c has MIC 0.5 ug/mL and ED50 14 mg/kg; discussion states it is five times more active than Papulacandin B
curator_note: 15c is the best NO.4 in vivo derivative in the current curated set.

hypothesis: Glucose position 6 substitution is possible but only selected cationic substitutions show useful in vivo activity
text_quote: Compound 17e has MIC 1 ug/mL and ED50 55 mg/kg
curator_note: Most other glucose-6 substitutions in Table 4 are less useful in vivo.

hypothesis: Improved water solubility alone is not enough to restore in vivo efficacy
text_quote: Compound 18b has MIC 0.5 ug/mL and ED50 220 mg/kg; discussion notes weak in vivo effect despite water solubility
curator_note: Useful caution for design work focused only on polarity or solubility.

hypothesis: Derivative optimization in this paper did not broaden the antifungal spectrum beyond Candida
text_quote: Authors state that no derivative had a wider spectrum including fungi other than Candida albicans
curator_note: Broader spectrum MIC columns were not expanded in v0.1 but the qualitative conclusion is captured here.

hypothesis: ACR1 ACR3 and ACR4 functions are needed for Papulacandin B whole-cell sensitivity
text_quote: LMF101 LMF105 and LMF106 have Papulacandin B MIC 50 ug/mL versus OL1 MIC 2.5 ug/mL
curator_note: Mechanism annotation not chemical SAR; useful for separating target inhibition from cell access.

hypothesis: ACR2 affects aculeacin A sensitivity but not Papulacandin B sensitivity
text_quote: LMF102 LMF103 and LMF104 have Papulacandin B MIC 2.5 ug/mL while aculeacin A MIC is 50 to >100 ug/mL
curator_note: This separates common echinocandin-like entry steps from Papulacandin B-specific behavior.

hypothesis: Conventional S. cerevisiae glucan synthase extract assay may miss Papulacandin B whole-cell sensitivity
text_quote: Papulacandin B up to 20 ug/mL did not have an inhibitory effect in vitro
curator_note: Do not merge this endpoint with Candida spheroplast glucan synthesis ED50 without an assay-context feature.

hypothesis: Loss of whole-cell susceptibility in acr mutants likely involves cell-surface accessibility rather than altered synthase
text_quote: Protoplasts from the four acr mutants were sensitive to aculeacin A and papulacandin B
curator_note: Supports the project framing that cell access and assay context matter.

hypothesis: Papulacandin B and aculeacin A share some entry or action steps but also have compound-specific steps
text_quote: Of 15 Papulacandin B-resistant mutants 10 were also resistant to aculeacin A while 5 remained susceptible
curator_note: Useful comparator logic for later FKS1 inhibitor resistance interpretation.

hypothesis: Papulacandin B strongly inhibits S pombe wild-type glucan synthase in vitro at 50 ug/mL
text_quote: Wild-type 972 h- retained 11.9% activity at 37 C and 8.9% activity at 30 C with Papulacandin B 50 ug/mL
curator_note: Contrasts with the NO.5 S cerevisiae extract negative result and shows why species and assay system must stay separate.

hypothesis: cwg1-1 grown at restrictive temperature gives a Papulacandin B-resistant glucan synthase phenotype
text_quote: cwg1-1 grown at 37 C retained 73.3% activity with Papulacandin B while wild type retained 11.9%
curator_note: Mechanism annotation not chemical SAR; likely reflects altered particulate or membrane-associated enzyme component.

hypothesis: cwg1-1 resistance to Papulacandin B appears at 37 C but not at 30 C
text_quote: cwg1-1 retained 15.6% activity at 30 C but 73.3% activity at 37 C with Papulacandin B 50 ug/mL
curator_note: This is a genotype-by-growth-temperature effect and should not be treated as compound potency change.

hypothesis: cwg2-1 has reduced glucan synthase activity but remains Papulacandin B-sensitive
text_quote: cwg2-1 grown at 37 C retained 14.3% activity with Papulacandin B 50 ug/mL
curator_note: Reconstitution experiments indicate cwg2-1 affects the soluble component rather than the Papulacandin B-resistant phenotype.

hypothesis: cwg1 maps very close to the Papulacandin B-resistant pap1 locus but complementation suggests different mutations
text_quote: cwg1 was strongly linked to pap1 and complementation between pap1-8 and cwg1-1 indicated different mutations
curator_note: Useful for later resistance-genetics interpretation but not proof that cwg1 is the Papulacandin B target.

hypothesis: L-687781 preserves Papulacandin-like beta-1-3-glucan synthase inhibition and Candida MIC
text_quote: L-687781 MIC is 1 to 2 ug/mL for most Candida strains and glucan synthesis IC50 is about 0.5 uM
curator_note: Use as a distinct natural product scaffold not as a Papulacandin B derivative.

hypothesis: L-687781 has strong Candida activity but weak activity against Cryptococcus and C. guilliermondii
text_quote: L-687781 MIC values are 1 to 2 ug/mL for most Candida strains but >128 ug/mL for C. guilliermondii and Cryptococcus neoformans
curator_note: Paper also states inactive against bacteria and filamentous fungi but those data were not shown.

hypothesis: Potent Candida MIC and target inhibition do not guarantee systemic Candida mouse efficacy
text_quote: L-687781 was active in vitro but treatment up to 10 mg/kg produced no significant efficacy in the C. albicans mouse model
curator_note: This directly supports the serum exposure PK and in vivo translation framing.

hypothesis: L-687781 can show in vivo activity in the rat PCP model despite weak Candida mouse efficacy
text_quote: L-687781 reduced P. carinii cyst burden by 72.6 to 83.5 percent at active doses in Table 2
curator_note: Model and pathogen context strongly affect apparent in vivo utility.

hypothesis: Dictyochaeta simplex fermentation can produce L-687781 at preparative scale
text_quote: Fermentation reached about 105 mg/L at 192 h and most compound was mycelial bound
curator_note: Production note for practical prioritization rather than chemical SAR.

modification: enzyme potency versus Candida TOKA translation gap
hypothesis: Strong C. albicans glucan synthase inhibition and broth MIC/MFC do not predict systemic Candida efficacy for L-687781 in TOKA
text_quote: L-687781 had IC50 0.16 uM but was ineffective at all concentrations tested in the TOKA.
curator_note: This is the cleanest NO.8 support for the corrected framing: cell MIC can be good while Candida in vivo translation is poor.

modification: lower enzyme IC50 but higher Candida MFC than echinocandin comparators
hypothesis: Target potency alone is not sufficient; killing and in vivo efficacy differ between papulacandin and echinocandin scaffolds
text_quote: The papulacandin showed the lowest IC50 but displayed higher MFCs for the Candida species than did echinocandins.
curator_note: Use as scaffold-class comparison, not a within-papulacandin substituent SAR claim.

effect_on_in_vivo: not evaluated for Cryptococcus in vivo
hypothesis: L-687781 activity is organism dependent, with Candida activity but weak Cryptococcus whole-cell activity
text_quote: The lipopeptides and the papulacandin L-687781 were inactive in vitro against the C. neoformans strains tested.
curator_note: The authors discuss glucan-linkage composition as a possible explanation; keep it as mechanistic context, not proof.

hypothesis: Poor Candida in vivo efficacy may involve PK bioavailability metabolism excretion or protein binding, but this paper does not measure those parameters
text_quote: Since pharmacokinetics and protein binding studies have not been conducted with papulacandin L-687781, whether these parameters contributed remains to be determined.
curator_note: Important caveat: record as a hypothesis/unknown, not as proven serum binding or PK failure.

modification: BU-4794F acyl side-chain variant versus L-687781
site: C10 and C16 acyl side chains
effect_on_mic: improved Candida MIC versus Papulacandin B/M comparators
hypothesis: BU-4794F keeps the L-687781-like papulacandin core but changes both acyl side chains, with potent Candida MIC and beta-1,3-glucan synthase inhibition
text_quote: Both compounds give very similar carbon signals in the spirocyclic core and two sugars moieties; major differences were observed in the two acyl side chains.
curator_note: Treat as family-level SAR; exact causal contribution of each acyl-chain change is not separated.

modification: BU-4794F compared with Papulacandin B and M
hypothesis: BU-4794F shows lower MIC values than Papulacandin B and M against the tested Candida strains
text_quote: The MIC values of BU-4794F against Candida strains are comparable to those of amphotericin B, but lower than those of papulacandins B and M.
curator_note: This is a direct potency comparison under one broth microdilution condition.

effect_on_mic: Candida/yeast active but Cryptococcus and Aspergillus inactive
hypothesis: BU-4794F is narrow spectrum, active against tested yeasts/Candida but inactive against Cryptococcus neoformans and Aspergillus fumigatus
text_quote: BU-4794F exhibits potent inhibitory activity against yeasts but is inactive against filamentous fungi and Cryptococcus neoformans.
curator_note: Aspergillus was represented by A. fumigatus IAM2034 only in this table.

hypothesis: BU-4794F is practically insoluble in water, so formulation/exposure should be checked before assuming in vivo translation
text_quote: It was soluble in lower alcohols and dimethyl sulfoxide ... but practically insoluble in hexane and water.
curator_note: This paper has no in vivo efficacy or serum/protein-binding data.

modification: C8 short side chain in Mer-WF3010
hypothesis: A C8 short fatty-acid side chain is sufficient for strong Candida whole-cell activity in this papulacandin-family member
text_quote: The short side chain fatty acid of Mer-WF3010 is shorter than that of any other known papulacandin, but the activity against Candida is as high as that of papulacandin B.
curator_note: Important side-chain length SAR; enzyme inhibition was not measured directly in this paper.

hypothesis: Mer-WF3010 killing depends on fungal growth state, consistent with cell-wall synthesis targeting
text_quote: Mer-WF3010 is fungicidal to growing cells of Candida albicans, but is inactive on resting cells.
curator_note: Treat as mode-of-action context; not a quantified MIC endpoint.

effect_on_mic: Candida active but Cryptococcus and Aspergillus inactive
hypothesis: Mer-WF3010 shows Candida activity with weak activity against Cryptococcus and Aspergillus in Table 2
text_quote: Mer-WF3010 has potent antifungal activity against Candida sp., being about as active as papulacandin B.
curator_note: Table 2 reports >40 ug/mL for Cryptococcus neoformans and Aspergillus fumigatus.

hypothesis: Mer-WF3010 had no observed acute toxic symptoms after high-dose intraperitoneal administration, but efficacy and PK were not evaluated
text_quote: Mer-WF3010 did not produce any toxic symptoms in mice at a dose of 1000 mg/kg when administered intraperitoneally.
curator_note: Do not interpret this as efficacy or exposure proof.

modification: Fusacandin A versus Fusacandin B potency
effect_on_mic: Fusacandin A much stronger than Fusacandin B in Table 1 and Table 2
hypothesis: Fusacandin B is a weaker minor congener while Fusacandin A is the main active congener
text_quote: Fusacandin A is considerably more active than fusacandin B.
curator_note: Do not infer the structural driver until companion structure paper is curated.

effect_on_mic: MIC shifts to >100 ug/mL in 50% mouse serum
hypothesis: Fusacandin A and Papulacandin B both lose apparent activity in 50% mouse serum under this assay condition
text_quote: The MICs of both fusacandin A and papulacandin B are greatly increased.
curator_note: This directly supports the user-corrected framing: serum-free activity can be reasonable while serum activity drops strongly.

modification: glucan synthase and whole-cell beta-glucan labeling
effect_on_enzyme_activity: Fusacandin A inhibits GS but less than Papulacandin B
effect_on_mic: retains Candida MIC in no-serum broth
hypothesis: Fusacandin A has target-linked beta-glucan synthesis inhibition by enzyme curve and whole-cell radiolabeling
text_quote: Fusacandin A was shown by two methods to be an inhibitor of (1,3)-beta-glucan synthesis.
curator_note: Fig. 6 lacks tabulated IC50; keep enzyme values approximate and emphasize direction.

hypothesis: Osmotic protection by sorbitol supports a fungal cell-wall target for Fusacandin and Papulacandin B activity
text_quote: The presence of sorbitol antagonized the inhibition of Candida albicans.
curator_note: Cilofungin and amphotericin B controls were not curated as compounds under the Papulacandin-only v0.1 boundary.

effect_on_enzyme_activity: Saricandin much weaker GS IC50 than PF-1042
effect_on_mic: Saricandin weaker Candida CCH 442 MIC than PF-1042
hypothesis: The C6-prime short acyl side chain identity strongly affects whole-cell and enzyme potency in this matched papulacandin pair
text_quote: The activity of saricandin was weaker.
curator_note: Pair is structurally close by Fig. 1, but still treat this as literature-derived SAR inference rather than a designed single-site analog experiment.

modification: PF-1042 potency relative to Fusacandin A and Papulacandin B
effect_on_enzyme_activity: PF-1042 GS IC50 6.5 ug/mL, between Papulacandin B and Fusacandin A in Table 2
effect_on_mic: PF-1042 MIC 0.49 ug/mL, comparable to Fusacandin A and Papulacandin B
hypothesis: PF-1042/BE-29602 preserves strong no-serum Candida activity and beta-glucan synthase inhibition in this assay panel
text_quote: PF-1042 was comparable to fusacandin A and papulacandin B.
curator_note: Do not interpret as serum or in vivo translation evidence; Table 2 is no-serum plus sorbitol protection and enzyme IC50.

modification: sorbitol protection phenotype across papulacandin-related compounds
effect_on_enzyme_activity: higher Saricandin MIC paralleled by higher GS IC50
effect_on_mic: 0.8 M sorbitol rescues growth for saricandin and related compounds
hypothesis: Sorbitol antagonism supports a fungal cell-wall mode of action for saricandin and co-tested papulacandin-related compounds
text_quote: Sorbitol could rescue the growth of cells.
curator_note: This is not a serum effect; record separately from the 50% mouse serum liability observed in NO.12.

modification: chaetiacandin-type benzyl alcohol core without spirocyclic moiety
effect_on_mic: retains marked Candida activity despite lacking the papulacandin B spirocyclic moiety
hypothesis: The classical spirocyclic moiety is not strictly required for strong Candida whole-cell activity, but organism spectrum remains limited
text_quote: furanocandin is of the chaetiacandin type with no spirocyclic moiety.
curator_note: This is a rational interpretation combining the structure text and Table 1 activity; no enzyme data were reported in this paper.

effect_on_mic: active against Candida species but weak against Cryptococcus and Aspergillus in Table 1
hypothesis: Galactofuranose-containing papulacandin expands sugar-core diversity for future SAR but does not by itself establish broader spectrum
text_quote: Furanocandin is the compound shown to possess a galactofuranosyl unit.
curator_note: Treat as scaffold-diversity evidence; do not over-interpret causal activity contribution without matched analogs.

effect_on_mic: strong Candida activity but Cryptococcus neoformans and Aspergillus fumigatus MIC >100 ug/mL
hypothesis: Furanocandin supports the recurring Papulacandin-family pattern of strong yeast/Candida activity with limited activity against organisms less dependent or less accessible to the same cell-wall target context
text_quote: MIC data are given in Table 1.
curator_note: Amphotericin B comparator values were not curated as database compound rows.

site: C3 long acyl chain and C6' short acyl chain
effect_on_enzyme_activity: not directly tested in this paper
effect_on_mic: retains potent yeast and Candida activity
hypothesis: BE-29602 preserves a papulacandin-like active profile despite a chaetiacandin-like C10 short side chain and missing C8/C14 methyl groups on the long side chain
text_quote: BE-29602 has a spirocyclic diglycoside, but its C10 side chain at C-6' position is identical to that of chaetiacandin.
curator_note: This is cross-natural-product SAR context, not a designed one-change analog experiment.

effect_on_enzyme_activity: expected beta-1,3-glucan synthesis inhibition but not measured directly
effect_on_mic: strong against Candida/Saccharomyces/Schizosaccharomyces but weak or incomplete against several filamentous fungi and bacteria
hypothesis: BE-29602 supports the recurring Papulacandin-family pattern of potent yeast activity with limited broad antibacterial or mold activity
text_quote: BE-29602 showed potent growth inhibition against several yeasts.
curator_note: Do not enter non-Papulacandin organisms as compounds; they are assay organisms only.

effect_on_mic: Candida MIC values are far below P388 IC50 in this paper
hypothesis: BE-29602 has a useful in vitro fungal-versus-P388 margin in no-serum antimicrobial agar assays, but this is not PK or in vivo efficacy evidence
text_quote: The IC50 was 21 ug/ml.
curator_note: Keep cytotoxicity separate from serum-shift liability and animal efficacy translation.

effect_on_mic: Fusacandin A and Papulacandin B retain no-serum Candida MIC but shift to >100 ug/mL in 50% mouse serum
hypothesis: Parent papulacandin/fusacandin whole-cell activity can be good without serum, but serum/protein binding sharply reduces apparent activity and may explain poor in vivo conversion.
text_quote: fusacandin was highly protein bound such that in the presence of serum its MIC increased from 0.78 ug/mL to >100 ug/mL.
curator_note: This reinforces the user-corrected framing; do not describe the class as simply enzyme-active but cell-inactive.

modification: C-6' side chain removal or minimal acetyl replacement
effect_on_mic: Fusacandin B loses potency and acetyl analog 6b is inactive against all five C. albicans strains
hypothesis: A substantial C-6' lipophilic side chain is required for whole-cell Candida MIC in this fusacandin series.
text_quote: the absence of the C-6' sidechain caused a great loss in activity; however simple acyl derivatives such as the acetyl analog 6b were also devoid of activity.
curator_note: This is causal within a semisynthetic series and stronger than cross-natural-product comparison.

effect_on_mic: Decanoyl 6c and related aliphatic analogs regain some no-serum MIC but remain inactive in 50% serum
hypothesis: Lengthening the aliphatic C-6' acyl chain helps no-serum whole-cell potency but does not solve serum liability.
text_quote: As the chain was lengthened e.g. decanoyl compound 6c, activity improved. Unfortunately, these analogs were inactive in the presence of serum.
curator_note: Keep no-serum and 50% serum rows separate during modeling.

effect_on_mic: Fused aromatic analogs 6f-i become the first derivatives with MIC <100 ug/mL in 50% mouse serum; 6h gives 25 ug/mL across strains
hypothesis: Rigid aromatic replacement at C-6' can reduce the severity of serum-associated activity loss while retaining Candida MIC.
text_quote: Expanding the aromatic nucleus led to compounds 6f-i which... were the first derivatives to present activity (<100 ug/mL) in presence of mouse serum assay.
curator_note: 6h is a useful benchmark for serum-retained MIC but not an in vivo/PK proof.

effect_on_mic: Biphenyl/terphenyl and alkoxy-biphenyl analogs show 12.5-25 ug/mL serum MIC values for many strains; authors identify 6n as most active
effect_on_in_vivo: further PK and in vivo study ongoing
hypothesis: The biphenyl C-6' ester template is a prioritized scaffold for serum-retained Candida MIC in this paper.
text_quote: The methoxybiphenyl analog 6n was seen as the most active compound in this series and further study... is ongoing.
curator_note: The table also shows several close analogs with similar serum MICs; treat 6n as author-prioritized, not uniquely dominant by every strain.

modification: overlong alkoxy chain or phenolic hydroxy analog
effect_on_mic: Octyloxy 6q and hydroxy 6u are weaker, especially in serum; methoxy-position regioisomers 6s/6t do not beat 6n
hypothesis: There is an apparent size and substituent-position optimum in the C-6' biphenyl series; simply adding polarity or long hydrophobes is not enough.
text_quote: Lengthening of the alkoxy chain... was compatible with retention of activity until the length approached that of the octyloxy analog 6q.
curator_note: This SAR is useful for rational side-chain design after exact structures are manually encoded.

modification: corynecandin versus Papulacandin B activity profile
effect_on_enzyme_activity: Corynecandin IC50 12.9 ug/mL versus Papulacandin B 1.9 ug/mL
effect_on_mic: Corynecandin MIC 0.98 ug/mL versus Papulacandin B 1.96 ug/mL in the same C. albicans CCH 442 assay
hypothesis: Corynecandin retains strong whole-cell Candida activity despite weaker beta-glucan synthase IC50 than Papulacandin B in this table.
text_quote: Corynecandin was found to have similar potency as an antifungal agent to several other papulacandin-type compounds.
curator_note: This supports separating whole-cell MIC from enzyme IC50 rather than collapsing them into one potency label.

effect_on_mic: MIC shifts from 0.98 ug/mL without sorbitol to >250 ug/mL with 0.8 M sorbitol
hypothesis: Sorbitol rescue supports a fungal cell-wall synthesis/assembly mode of action for Corynecandin, but it should not be interpreted as serum protein binding.
text_quote: Sorbitol could rescue the growth of cells when treated with corynecandin indicating that this compound also inhibits fungal cell wall synthesis and assembly.
curator_note: Keep this separate from mouse-serum or human-serum activity-loss rows.

modification: shorter C3 ester chain relative to chaetiacandin
hypothesis: A shortened or unusual C3 ester chain can still support antifungal and glucan synthase activity in a papulacandin-type glycolipid.
text_quote: The ester chain found at C3 is not found in other glycolipids and corynecandin is the smallest known member of this class to retain antifungal and glucan synthase inhibitory activity.
curator_note: This is cross-natural-product SAR context, not a one-change analog experiment.

modification: chaetiacandin-type aryl glycoside without classical spiroketal
hypothesis: Corynecandin reinforces that chaetiacandin-type papulacandin-family scaffolds lacking the classical Papulacandin B spiroketal can retain activity.

hypothesis: A protected 3-formylphenyl glucoside intermediate showed only borderline MIC50 activity, so protecting-group hydrophobicity alone is not enough to generate strong Papulacandin-like activity.
text_quote: Table 69 reports substance 7 200 ug/mL against Candida albicans and >200 ug/mL against the other listed Candida species.
curator_note: This is a synthetic intermediate, retained because the thesis activity table explicitly tested it and it remains a Papulacandin D related aryl glucoside.

modification: simplified aryl O-glycoside analog 1 with aromatic cinnamoylamide side chain
site: spiroketal core replaced; glucose O-3 side chain
hypothesis: The simplified aryl O-glycoside analog 1 did not retain measurable whole-cell antifungal activity at 500 ug/mL despite the intended Papulacandin D molecular-simplification design.
text_quote: Section 5.2 states that none of the evaluated compounds was active up to 500 ug/mL.
curator_note: This thesis source supports the later negative result for PAPU-0125/compound 1 and adds tested intermediate context.

site: glucose O-4/O-6 protection; glucose O-3 ester retained
hypothesis: Keeping the 4,6-O-benzylidene protecting group on the C3 ester precursor did not generate antifungal activity at the 500 ug/mL threshold.
text_quote: Section 5.2 states intermediates 5-8 were also evaluated and no compound was active up to 500 ug/mL.
curator_note: Use as negative whole-cell evidence for protected intermediate 8 only; no enzyme assay was reported.

modification: move aromatic cadeia 12 ester from glucose O-3 to O-2
hypothesis: The C2 ester regioisomer 8.1 was also inactive at 500 ug/mL, so this source does not support a favorable O-2 versus O-3 whole-cell activity preference.
text_quote: Section 5.2 explicitly includes the C-2 ester 8.1 among evaluated compounds and states that none was active up to 500 ug/mL.
curator_note: This is a negative positional-SAR observation only; all tested values are censored at the same threshold.

modification: add a second aromatic cadeia 12 ester to form protected 2,3-diester 8.2
hypothesis: The bulkier protected 2,3-diester 8.2 was inactive at 500 ug/mL, so increasing aromatic side-chain loading did not rescue whole-cell activity.
text_quote: Section 5.2 explicitly includes di-ester 8.2 among evaluated compounds and states that none was active up to 500 ug/mL.
curator_note: Protected diester side product related to later deprotected di-cadeia analogs; full SMILES still needs manual drawing.
